question: Which of the following best describes the topology/structure of the World Wide Web?
options: It is a complex structure containing all topology types
explanation: The WWW has a complex topology that contains all types: star-shaped, grid-shaped, tree-shaped, ring-shaped, and fully-connected.
simple_explanation: Think of the web like a city road network — it's not a neat grid or a simple ring. It's a messy combination of all those shapes mixed together.

question: In a traditional 3-tier web architecture, which technologies run on the CLIENT tier?
explanation: The client tier (browser) runs HTML + CSS + JavaScript. The server tier runs PHP/Java/etc. and the database tier runs MySQL/Oracle/etc.
simple_explanation: A web app has 3 layers: your browser (HTML/CSS/JS), a server (PHP/Java), and a database (MySQL). The client tier is just what runs inside your browser.

question: In the separation of concerns principle applied to client-side technologies, what is the role of CSS?
explanation: In client-side technologies: HTML = content, CSS = style and appearance, JavaScript = action. This principle was introduced by Edsger Dijkstra.
simple_explanation: HTML is the words on a page, CSS is what makes it look nice (colours, fonts, layout), and JavaScript makes things happen when you click. CSS = looks.

question: Which HTML attribute is used to give an element a unique identifier?
explanation: All HTML tags share these global attributes: id (unique identifier), title (tooltip text), style (inline CSS), and class (one or more CSS class names).
simple_explanation: The 'id' attribute is like a name tag — it's unique to one specific element on the page. Only one element can have a given id, so you can target it directly.

question: Which HTML tag is used to create a hyperlink?
explanation: The <a> tag creates a hyperlink. Its main attribute is href, which specifies the URL — either absolute (e.g. http://www.google.com) or relative (e.g. 1/test.html).
simple_explanation: The tag stands for 'anchor'. You put the destination URL in its href attribute and the clickable text between the tags: Click here.

question: In an HTML table, which tag is used to create a HEADER cell?
explanation: In an HTML table: <table> defines the table, <tr> defines a row, <th> defines a header cell (bold by default), and <td> defines a data cell.
simple_explanation: Think of a spreadsheet: <th> is for the column headers (bold text), and <td> is for the regular data cells. <tr> wraps each row.

question: An HTML Element is defined as:
explanation:
An Element = Tag + Content. For example:

Hello

— the opening tag, the text content, and the closing tag together form the element.
simple_explanation:
A tag is the label (

), content is what's inside (Hello), and the whole thing together —

Hello

— is the element.

question: Which of the following is TRUE about HTML tag case sensitivity?
options:
Tags are not case sensitive —

and

are the same
Only attribute names are case sensitive

Tags are case sensitive —

,

, and

are treated differently
Only closing tags are case sensitive
explanation:
HTML tags are case sensitive.

,

, and

are treated as different tags, which causes many errors. Best practice is to always use lowercase.
simple_explanation:
In HTML,

and

are NOT the same thing. Using the wrong case causes bugs. Just always write your tags in lowercase to be safe.

question: What is the correct way to start a PHP code block?
explanation: PHP code blocks begin with <?php and end with ?>. These are called PHP declaration blocks.
simple_explanation: PHP code is wrapped in <?php ... ?> tags — like putting your code inside a special container so the server knows to run it as PHP.

question: When does PHP code execute?
options: In the browser, after the page loads
On the web server, before the page is sent to the client
On the client, as the user interacts with the page
Only when the user submits a form
explanation: PHP code executes on a web server BEFORE the web page is sent to a client. The generated HTML is what gets returned to the browser.
simple_explanation: PHP runs on the server before you see anything in your browser. The server processes all the PHP first, then sends plain HTML to your browser. Your browser never sees the PHP code itself.

question: What character do PHP variable names start with?
explanation: All PHP variables start with a $ sign, for example: $votingAge, $name, $count.
simple_explanation: Every PHP variable starts with a $ sign. It's how PHP knows it's a variable and not just a word. So $name is a variable, but name is not.

question: What is the correct file extension for a PHP file that contains PHP code?
explanation: .php is the default extension that most web servers use to process PHP scripts. A PHP file that contains NO PHP code should use .html instead.
simple_explanation: Just like Word documents end in .docx, PHP files end in .php. The server uses this extension to know it needs to run the PHP engine on that file.

question:
What will the following PHP code output? echo '

The legal voting age is $votingAge.

';
explanation: When a variable is surrounded by SINGLE quotation marks, the literal text '$votingAge' is printed — the variable is NOT interpolated. Double quotes would print the variable's value.
simple_explanation: Single quotes in PHP are 'what you see is what you get'. They print everything exactly as typed, including the dollar sign and variable name. No magic happens.

question:
What will the following PHP code output if $votingAge = 18? echo "

The legal voting age is $votingAge.

";
explanation: When a variable is surrounded by DOUBLE quotation marks, PHP looks up the variable's value and inserts it. So $votingAge gets replaced with 18 in the output.
simple_explanation: Double quotes are 'smart' — PHP looks inside them for any $ variables and swaps them out for their actual values. Single quotes don't do this.

question: What are the two types of PHP arrays?
explanation: PHP has indexed arrays (accessed by a numeric index, starting at 0) and associative arrays (accessed by a named key).
simple_explanation: Indexed arrays are like a numbered list (item 0, item 1, item 2...). Associative arrays are like a dictionary — each item has a name (key) instead of a number.

question: An array in PHP is best described as:
options: A function that returns multiple values
A set of data represented by a single variable name
explanation: An array contains a set of data represented by a single variable name. For example, $provinces[] could hold all province names.
simple_explanation: An array is a box that holds multiple values under one variable name. Instead of $city1, $city2, $city3, you just have one $cities array with all of them inside.

question: Which PHP operator type compares operands and returns a Boolean value?
explanation: Comparison operators compare operands and return a Boolean value (true/false). Arithmetic operators perform math, assignment operators assign values, and string operators perform operations on strings.
simple_explanation: Comparison operators ask a yes/no question — like 'is 5 greater than 3?' and return either true or false. Examples: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=

question: What does the += operator do in PHP?
options: Checks if two values are equal
Combines the right operand value with the left operand and assigns the result to the left operand
Divides the left operand by the right operand
Appends a string to another string
explanation: += is the compound addition assignment operator. It adds the right operand to the left operand and assigns the new value back to the left operand.
simple_explanation: $x += 5 is shorthand for $x = $x + 5. It takes whatever $x currently is, adds 5 to it, and saves the result back into $x.

question: A UNARY operator requires:
options: An operand before AND after the operator
A single operand either before or after the operator
explanation: A unary operator requires a single operand, either before or after the operator (e.g. $count++ or ++$count). A binary operator requires an operand both before and after the operator.
simple_explanation: Unary means 'one'. A unary operator only needs one value to work — like $count++ which just adds 1 to $count. No second value needed.

question: What does the $_GET superglobal contain?
options: An array of values passed via HTTP cookies
An array of values from a form submitted with the GET method
explanation: $_GET contains an array of values from a form submitted with the GET method. $_POST does the same for POST. $_SESSION holds session variables, and $_COOKIE holds cookie values.
simple_explanation: $_GET grabs data sent via the URL. When you search Google and see ?q=cats in the address bar, PHP would read that 'cats' using $_GET['q'].

question: Which superglobal array contains values from BOTH $_GET and $_POST (and cookies)?
explanation: $_REQUEST is an array of all elements found in $_COOKIE, $_GET, and $_POST arrays combined.
simple_explanation: $_REQUEST is the lazy option — it combines $_GET, $_POST, and $_COOKIE into one array so you don't have to care how the data was sent.

question: What does the $_SESSION superglobal contain?
options: An array of information about uploaded files
An array of values passed as HTTP cookies
An array of session variables available to the current script
explanation: $_SESSION is an array of session variables that are available to the current script. Sessions allow data to persist across multiple page requests for the same user.
simple_explanation: $_SESSION lets you store info about a user that sticks around as they browse different pages — like keeping them logged in or remembering items in their cart.

question: Which PHP function is used to test whether a variable contains a numeric string?
explanation: is_numeric() tests whether a variable contains a numeric string (or a numeric value). It is commonly used to validate form input before performing calculations.
simple_explanation: is_numeric() asks 'is this a number?' — useful when someone fills in a form and you want to make sure they typed a number before doing maths with it.

question: When validating a PHP form, which function is used to check that a field value is NOT empty?
explanation: !empty() checks that a variable has a value. empty() returns true if the variable is empty, so !empty() means 'this variable is NOT empty' — used to ensure form fields were filled in.
simple_explanation: empty() checks if something is blank. The ! flips the result — so !empty() means 'this field has something in it'. Use it before processing form data to avoid errors.

question: How many parameters does the substr() function take?
explanation: substr() takes three parameters: (1) the string to take a substring of, (2) the start position, (3) the length to select. If the third parameter is blank, it selects until the end of the string.
simple_explanation: substr() cuts a piece out of a string. You tell it: which string, where to start cutting, and how many characters to take. Leave out the third parameter to grab everything from the start position onwards.

question: What function can substr() be combined with to find the position of a substring?
explanation: substr() is often combined with strpos() to find occurrences within a string. strpos() returns the position (index) of the first occurrence of a substring.
simple_explanation: strpos() is like Ctrl+F — it searches inside a string and tells you the position (index number) where it found the match. You then use substr() to extract that part.

question: What does ASCII stand for?
options: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
Advanced System Code for Internet Interchange
Automated Standard Code for Information Input
explanation: ASCII stands for American Standard Code for Information Interchange. It provides numeric representations of English characters, with values ranging from 0 to 255.
simple_explanation: ASCII is a system that gives every letter, number, and symbol a unique number so computers can store and compare them. 'A' is 65, 'a' is 97, '0' is 48, and so on.

question: In ASCII, why are lowercase letters evaluated as 'greater than' uppercase letters when compared?
options: Lowercase letters were added to ASCII later
Lowercase letters have HIGHER numeric values (97–122) than uppercase (65–90)
Uppercase letters are stored differently in memory
It depends on the PHP version
explanation: Lowercase letters (values 97–122) have higher ASCII numeric values than uppercase letters (values 65–90). Since string comparison is based on these values, lowercase letters are evaluated as 'greater'.
simple_explanation: Computers compare letters by their ASCII number. 'a' = 97, 'A' = 65. Since 97 > 65, the lowercase 'a' is considered 'bigger' than uppercase 'A' when compared.

question: When does the else clause execute in an if...else statement?
options: When the if condition is true
When the if condition is false
Only when the if block has an error
explanation: The else clause executes when the condition in the if...else statement evaluates to FALSE. If the condition is true, only the if block runs.
simple_explanation: Think of it like: 'if it's raining, take an umbrella — else, leave it at home.' The else only happens when the if condition wasn't true.

question: What is the key difference between a while loop and a do...while loop?
options: while loops run faster than do...while loops
do...while loops cannot use a counter variable
A do...while loop always executes at least once before checking the condition
A while loop can only count up, not down
explanation: do...while statements always execute once before the conditional expression is evaluated. A while loop checks the condition BEFORE each iteration, so it may never execute if the condition starts as false.
simple_explanation: A while loop checks first, then runs. A do...while runs first, then checks. The difference? A do...while is GUARANTEED to execute at least once, even if the condition is already false.

explanation: The loop runs while $count <= 5, starting at 1 and incrementing each time with $count++. It executes for values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — a total of 5 times.
simple_explanation: Start at 1, keep going while 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 are all ≤ 5 — that's 5 rounds. When $count becomes 6, the condition (6 ≤ 5) is false, so the loop stops.

options: 0 times — the condition is immediately false
1 time — it runs once before checking the condition
explanation: $count starts at 2 and the condition is ($count < 2). A regular while loop would never execute since 2 < 2 is false. But do...while always runs the body ONCE first, then checks — so it executes exactly once.
simple_explanation: Normally 2 < 2 is false so nothing would run. But do...while always runs at least once before checking the condition. So the body runs once, $count becomes 3, the condition (3 < 2) fails, and it stops.

question: The syntax of PHP control structures (if, while, for, switch) is most similar to which other programming languages?
explanation: PHP control structures are very similar to Java and C. This is why the syntax (if, while, for, do...while, switch) looks familiar to developers with a Java or C background.
simple_explanation: If you've done Java or C before, PHP's if/while/for loops will look almost identical. The curly brace style and syntax are borrowed from those languages.

question: In the Ajax Technology Family, which role does 'fetch' play?
explanation: In the Ajax Technology Family: HTML+CSS = standards-based presentation, DOM = dynamic display/interaction, JSON = data interchange, fetch = asynchronous data retrieval, JavaScript = binding everything together.
simple_explanation: fetch is the modern JavaScript tool for requesting data from a server in the background — without reloading the page. It 'fetches' data asynchronously (quietly, behind the scenes).

question: What does DOM stand for?
explanation: DOM stands for Document Object Model. It is used for dynamic display and interaction on the client, and for managing JSON/XML on both client and server.
simple_explanation: The DOM is like a live map of your webpage that JavaScript can read and change. When you click a button and text appears without reloading — that's JavaScript editing the DOM.

explanation: In JavaScript, object properties are accessed with dot notation: objectName.propertyName. So who.name accesses the 'name' property of the 'who' object, which holds 'Chuck'.
simple_explanation: In JavaScript, to get a property from an object you write: objectName.propertyName. So who.name gives you whatever is stored in the 'name' field — which is 'Chuck'.

question: What does WSDL stand for?
explanation: WSDL stands for Web Service Description Language. It is used to describe web services in a standardised format — defining what operations the service offers and what data it expects.
simple_explanation: WSDL is like a menu for a web service — it describes what the service can do, what info you need to send it, and what it'll send back. It's a formal description document.

question: Which term best describes how AJAX handles user interactions on a web page?
explanation: The Ajax application model is a 'partial screen update' model — only the interface elements containing new information are updated; the rest of the page remains displayed without interruption.
simple_explanation: AJAX only updates the part of the page that changed. Like on Facebook — when a new comment appears, the whole page doesn't reload. Just that section updates quietly.

question: How does AJAX achieve its 'partial screen update' model?
options: By reloading the entire HTML page
By submitting a form to the server
Through dynamic change of the internal representation of the document displayed in the browser
By refreshing only the CSS stylesheet
explanation: The partial update is achieved through dynamic change of the DOM — the internal representation of the document in the browser — using JavaScript, without a full page reload.
simple_explanation: AJAX updates the page by changing the DOM (the browser's internal map of the page) using JavaScript. No server round-trip needed — it just edits what's already on screen.

question: In JSON, which data type is used to hold an ordered list of multiple values?
explanation: JSON supports multiple data types: String, Integer, Boolean, Object, and List/Array. A List/Array holds multiple ordered values, for example: "offices": ["3350DMC", "3437NQ"].
simple_explanation: In JSON, square brackets [ ] mean a list/array — it holds multiple values in order. Like "colours": ["red", "blue", "green"] — three values stored as one list.

question: Which of the following server-side languages is part of the Ajax Technology Family for handling server interaction?
explanation: PHP is part of the Ajax Technology Family alongside ASP.NET and Java. A server-side language is needed to handle interactions with the server and database.
simple_explanation: AJAX is mostly front-end, but when it needs to talk to a server or database, it needs a back-end language. PHP is the one used in this course for that role.
